speaker: PORTIA
text: I pray you tarry, pause a day or two

speaker: PORTIA
text: Before you hazard, for in choosing wrong

speaker: PORTIA
text: I lose your company; therefore forbear a while.

speaker: PORTIA
text: There’s something tells me (but it is not love)

speaker: PORTIA
text: I would not lose you, and you know yourself

speaker: PORTIA
text: Hate counsels not in such a quality.

speaker: PORTIA
text: But lest you should not understand me well

speaker: PORTIA
text: (And yet a maiden hath no tongue but thought)

speaker: PORTIA
text: I would detain you here some month or two

speaker: PORTIA
text: Before you venture for me. I could teach you

speaker: PORTIA
text: How to choose right, but then I am forsworn.

speaker: PORTIA
text: So will I never be. So may you miss me.

speaker: PORTIA
text: But if you do, you’ll make me wish a sin,

speaker: PORTIA
text: That I had been forsworn. Beshrew your eyes,

speaker: PORTIA
text: They have o’erlooked me and divided me.

speaker: PORTIA
text: One half of me is yours, the other half yours—

speaker: PORTIA
text: Mine own, I would say—but if mine, then yours,

speaker: PORTIA
text: And so all yours. O, these naughty times

speaker: PORTIA
text: Puts bars between the owners and their rights!

speaker: PORTIA
text: And so though yours, not yours. Prove it so,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Let Fortune go to hell for it, not I.

speaker: PORTIA
text: I speak too long, but ’tis to peize the time,

speaker: PORTIA
text: To eche it, and to draw it out in length,

speaker: PORTIA
text: To stay you from election.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Let me choose,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: For as I am, I live upon the rack.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Upon the rack, Bassanio? Then confess

speaker: PORTIA
text: What treason there is mingled with your love.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: None but that ugly treason of mistrust,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Which makes me fear th’ enjoying of my love.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: There may as well be amity and life

speaker: BASSANIO
text: ’Tween snow and fire, as treason and my love.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Ay, but I fear you speak upon the rack

speaker: PORTIA
text: Where men enforcèd do speak anything.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Promise me life and I’ll confess the truth.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Well, then, confess and live.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: “Confess and love”

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Had been the very sum of my confession.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: O happy torment, when my torturer

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Doth teach me answers for deliverance!

speaker: BASSANIO
text: But let me to my fortune and the caskets.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Away, then. I am locked in one of them.

speaker: PORTIA
text: If you do love me, you will find me out.—

speaker: PORTIA
text: Nerissa and the rest, stand all aloof.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Let music sound while he doth make his choice.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Then if he lose he makes a swanlike end,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Fading in music. That the comparison

speaker: PORTIA
text: May stand more proper, my eye shall be the stream

speaker: PORTIA
text: And wat’ry deathbed for him. He may win,

speaker: PORTIA
text: And what is music then? Then music is

speaker: PORTIA
text: Even as the flourish when true subjects bow

speaker: PORTIA
text: To a new-crownèd monarch. Such it is

speaker: PORTIA
text: As are those dulcet sounds in break of day

speaker: PORTIA
text: That creep into the dreaming bridegroom’s ear

speaker: PORTIA
text: And summon him to marriage. Now he goes,

speaker: PORTIA
text: With no less presence but with much more love

speaker: PORTIA
text: Than young Alcides when he did redeem

speaker: PORTIA
text: The virgin tribute paid by howling Troy

speaker: PORTIA
text: To the sea-monster. I stand for sacrifice;

speaker: PORTIA
text: The rest aloof are the Dardanian wives,

speaker: PORTIA
text: With blearèd visages, come forth to view

speaker: PORTIA
text: The issue of th’ exploit. Go, Hercules!

speaker: PORTIA
text: Live thou, I live. With much much more dismay

speaker: PORTIA
text: I view the fight than thou that mak’st the fray.

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: Tell me where is fancy bred,

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: Or in the heart, or in the head?

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: How begot, how nourishèd?

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: Reply, reply.

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: It is engendered in the eye,

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: With gazing fed, and fancy dies

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: In the cradle where it lies.

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: Let us all ring fancy’s knell.

speaker: UNKNOWN
text: I’ll begin it.—Ding, dong, bell.

speaker: ALL
text: Ding, dong, bell.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: So may the outward shows be least themselves;

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The world is still deceived with ornament.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: In law, what plea so tainted and corrupt

speaker: BASSANIO
text: But, being seasoned with a gracious voice,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Obscures the show of evil? In religion,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: What damnèd error but some sober brow

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Will bless it and approve it with a text,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Hiding the grossness with fair ornament?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: There is no vice so simple but assumes

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Some mark of virtue on his outward parts.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: How many cowards whose hearts are all as false

speaker: BASSANIO
text: As stairs of sand, wear yet upon their chins

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The beards of Hercules and frowning Mars,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Who inward searched have livers white as milk,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And these assume but valor’s excrement

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To render them redoubted. Look on beauty,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And you shall see ’tis purchased by the weight,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Which therein works a miracle in nature,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Making them lightest that wear most of it.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: So are those crispèd snaky golden locks,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Which maketh such wanton gambols with the wind

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Upon supposèd fairness, often known

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To be the dowry of a second head,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The skull that bred them in the sepulcher.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Thus ornament is but the guilèd shore

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To a most dangerous sea, the beauteous scarf

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Veiling an Indian beauty; in a word,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The seeming truth which cunning times put on

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To entrap the wisest. Therefore, then, thou gaudy gold,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Hard food for Midas, I will none of thee.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Nor none of thee, thou pale and common drudge

speaker: BASSANIO
text: ’Tween man and man. But thou, thou meager lead,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Which rather threaten’st than dost promise aught,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Thy paleness moves me more than eloquence,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And here choose I. Joy be the consequence!

speaker: PORTIA
text: How all the other passions fleet to air,

speaker: PORTIA
text: As doubtful thoughts and rash embraced despair,

speaker: PORTIA
text: And shudd’ring fear, and green-eyed jealousy!

speaker: PORTIA
text: O love, be moderate, allay thy ecstasy,

speaker: PORTIA
text: In measure rain thy joy, scant this excess!

speaker: PORTIA
text: I feel too much thy blessing. Make it less,

speaker: PORTIA
text: For fear I surfeit.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: What find I here?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Fair Portia’s counterfeit! What demigod

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Hath come so near creation? Move these eyes?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Or whether, riding on the balls of mine,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Seem they in motion? Here are severed lips

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Parted with sugar breath; so sweet a bar

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Should sunder such sweet friends. Here in her hairs

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The painter plays the spider, and hath woven

speaker: BASSANIO
text: A golden mesh t’ entrap the hearts of men

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Faster than gnats in cobwebs. But her eyes!

speaker: BASSANIO
text: How could he see to do them? Having made one,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Methinks it should have power to steal both his

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And leave itself unfurnished. Yet look how far

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The substance of my praise doth wrong this shadow

speaker: BASSANIO
text: In underprizing it, so far this shadow

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Doth limp behind the substance. Here’s the scroll,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The continent and summary of my fortune.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: You that choose not by the view

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Chance as fair and choose as true.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Since this fortune falls to you,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Be content and seek no new.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: If you be well pleased with this

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And hold your fortune for your bliss,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Turn you where your lady is,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And claim her with a loving kiss.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: A gentle scroll! Fair lady, by your leave,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I come by note to give and to receive.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Like one of two contending in a prize

speaker: BASSANIO
text: That thinks he hath done well in people’s eyes,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Hearing applause and universal shout,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Giddy in spirit, still gazing in a doubt

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Whether those peals of praise be his or no,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: So, thrice-fair lady, stand I even so,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: As doubtful whether what I see be true,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Until confirmed, signed, ratified by you.

speaker: PORTIA
text: You see me, Lord Bassanio, where I stand,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Such as I am. Though for myself alone

speaker: PORTIA
text: I would not be ambitious in my wish

speaker: PORTIA
text: To wish myself much better, yet for you

speaker: PORTIA
text: I would be trebled twenty times myself,

speaker: PORTIA
text: A thousand times more fair, ten thousand times

speaker: PORTIA
text: More rich, that only to stand high in your account

speaker: PORTIA
text: I might in virtues, beauties, livings, friends,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Exceed account. But the full sum of me

speaker: PORTIA
text: Is sum of something, which, to term in gross,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Is an unlessoned girl, unschooled, unpracticed;

speaker: PORTIA
text: Happy in this, she is not yet so old

speaker: PORTIA
text: But she may learn; happier than this,

speaker: PORTIA
text: She is not bred so dull but she can learn;

speaker: PORTIA
text: Happiest of all, is that her gentle spirit

speaker: PORTIA
text: Commits itself to yours to be directed

speaker: PORTIA
text: As from her lord, her governor, her king.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Myself, and what is mine, to you and yours

speaker: PORTIA
text: Is now converted. But now I was the lord

speaker: PORTIA
text: Of this fair mansion, master of my servants,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Queen o’er myself; and even now, but now,

speaker: PORTIA
text: This house, these servants, and this same myself

speaker: PORTIA
text: Are yours, my lord’s. I give them with this ring,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Which, when you part from, lose, or give away,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Let it presage the ruin of your love,

speaker: PORTIA
text: And be my vantage to exclaim on you.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Madam, you have bereft me of all words.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Only my blood speaks to you in my veins,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And there is such confusion in my powers

speaker: BASSANIO
text: As after some oration fairly spoke

speaker: BASSANIO
text: By a belovèd prince there doth appear

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Among the buzzing pleasèd multitude,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Where every something being blent together

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Turns to a wild of nothing, save of joy

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Expressed and not expressed. But when this ring

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Parts from this finger, then parts life from hence.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: O, then be bold to say Bassanio’s dead!

speaker: NERISSA
text: My lord and lady, it is now our time,

speaker: NERISSA
text: That have stood by and seen our wishes prosper,

speaker: NERISSA
text: To cry “Good joy, good joy, my lord and lady!”

speaker: GRATIANO
text: My Lord Bassanio, and my gentle lady,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: I wish you all the joy that you can wish,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: For I am sure you can wish none from me.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: And when your honors mean to solemnize

speaker: GRATIANO
text: The bargain of your faith, I do beseech you

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Even at that time I may be married too.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: With all my heart, so thou canst get a wife.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: I thank your Lordship, you have got me one.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: My eyes, my lord, can look as swift as yours:

speaker: GRATIANO
text: You saw the mistress, I beheld the maid.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: You loved, I loved; for intermission

speaker: GRATIANO
text: No more pertains to me, my lord, than you.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Your fortune stood upon the caskets there,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: And so did mine, too, as the matter falls.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: For wooing here until I sweat again,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: And swearing till my very roof was dry

speaker: GRATIANO
text: With oaths of love, at last (if promise last)

speaker: GRATIANO
text: I got a promise of this fair one here

speaker: GRATIANO
text: To have her love, provided that your fortune

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Achieved her mistress.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Is this true, Nerissa?

speaker: NERISSA
text: Madam, it is, so you stand pleased withal.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And do you, Gratiano, mean good faith?

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Yes, faith, my lord.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Our feast shall be much honored in your marriage.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: We’ll play with them the first boy for a

speaker: GRATIANO
text: thousand ducats.

speaker: NERISSA
text: What, and stake down?

speaker: GRATIANO
text: No, we shall ne’er win at that sport and

speaker: GRATIANO
text: stake down.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: But who comes here? Lorenzo and his infidel?

speaker: GRATIANO
text: What, and my old Venetian friend Salerio?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Lorenzo and Salerio, welcome hither—

speaker: BASSANIO
text: If that the youth of my new int’rest here

speaker: BASSANIO
text:
Have power to bid you welcome.
                 
                To
                 
                Portia
                .
               By your leave,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I bid my very friends and countrymen,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Sweet Portia, welcome.

speaker: PORTIA
text: So do I, my lord. They are entirely welcome.

speaker: LORENZO
text: I thank your Honor. For my part, my lord,

speaker: LORENZO
text: My purpose was not to have seen you here,

speaker: LORENZO
text: But meeting with Salerio by the way,

speaker: LORENZO
text: He did entreat me past all saying nay

speaker: LORENZO
text: To come with him along.

speaker: SALERIO
text: I did, my lord,

speaker: SALERIO
text: And I have reason for it.

speaker: SALERIO
text: Signior Antonio

speaker: SALERIO
text: Commends him to you.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Ere I ope his letter,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I pray you tell me how my good friend doth.

speaker: SALERIO
text: Not sick, my lord, unless it be in mind,

speaker: SALERIO
text: Nor well, unless in mind. His letter there

speaker: SALERIO
text: Will show you his estate.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Nerissa, cheer yond stranger, bid her welcome.—

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Your hand, Salerio. What’s the news from Venice?

speaker: GRATIANO
text: How doth that royal merchant, good Antonio?

speaker: GRATIANO
text: I know he will be glad of our success.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: We are the Jasons, we have won the Fleece.

speaker: SALERIO
text: I would you had won the fleece that he hath lost.

speaker: PORTIA
text: There are some shrewd contents in yond same paper

speaker: PORTIA
text: That steals the color from Bassanio’s cheek.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Some dear friend dead, else nothing in the world

speaker: PORTIA
text: Could turn so much the constitution

speaker: PORTIA
text: Of any constant man. What, worse and worse?—

speaker: PORTIA
text: With leave, Bassanio, I am half yourself,

speaker: PORTIA
text: And I must freely have the half of anything

speaker: PORTIA
text: That this same paper brings you.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: O sweet Portia,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Here are a few of the unpleasant’st words

speaker: BASSANIO
text: That ever blotted paper. Gentle lady,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: When I did first impart my love to you,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I freely told you all the wealth I had

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Ran in my veins: I was a gentleman.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And then I told you true; and yet, dear lady,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Rating myself at nothing, you shall see

speaker: BASSANIO
text: How much I was a braggart. When I told you

speaker: BASSANIO
text: My state was nothing, I should then have told you

speaker: BASSANIO
text: That I was worse than nothing; for indeed

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I have engaged myself to a dear friend,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Engaged my friend to his mere enemy

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To feed my means. Here is a letter, lady,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The paper as the body of my friend,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And every word in it a gaping wound

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Issuing life blood.—But is it true, Salerio?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Hath all his ventures failed? What, not one hit?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: From Tripolis, from Mexico and England,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: From Lisbon, Barbary, and India,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And not one vessel ’scape the dreadful touch

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Of merchant-marring rocks?

speaker: SALERIO
text: Not one, my lord.

speaker: SALERIO
text: Besides, it should appear that if he had

speaker: SALERIO
text: The present money to discharge the Jew,

speaker: SALERIO
text: He would not take it. Never did I know

speaker: SALERIO
text: A creature that did bear the shape of man

speaker: SALERIO
text: So keen and greedy to confound a man.

speaker: SALERIO
text: He plies the Duke at morning and at night,

speaker: SALERIO
text: And doth impeach the freedom of the state

speaker: SALERIO
text: If they deny him justice. Twenty merchants,

speaker: SALERIO
text: The Duke himself, and the magnificoes

speaker: SALERIO
text: Of greatest port have all persuaded with him,

speaker: SALERIO
text: But none can drive him from the envious plea

speaker: SALERIO
text: Of forfeiture, of justice, and his bond.

speaker: JESSICA
text: When I was with him, I have heard him swear

speaker: JESSICA
text: To Tubal and to Chus, his countrymen,

speaker: JESSICA
text: That he would rather have Antonio’s flesh

speaker: JESSICA
text: Than twenty times the value of the sum

speaker: JESSICA
text: That he did owe him. And I know, my lord,

speaker: JESSICA
text: If law, authority, and power deny not,

speaker: JESSICA
text: It will go hard with poor Antonio.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Is it your dear friend that is thus in trouble?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The dearest friend to me, the kindest man,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The best conditioned and unwearied spirit

speaker: BASSANIO
text: In doing courtesies, and one in whom

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The ancient Roman honor more appears

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Than any that draws breath in Italy.

speaker: PORTIA
text: What sum owes he the Jew?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: For me, three thousand ducats.

speaker: PORTIA
text: What, no more?

speaker: PORTIA
text: Pay him six thousand and deface the bond.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Double six thousand and then treble that,

speaker: PORTIA
text: Before a friend of this description

speaker: PORTIA
text: Shall lose a hair through Bassanio’s fault.

speaker: PORTIA
text: First go with me to church and call me wife,

speaker: PORTIA
text: And then away to Venice to your friend!

speaker: PORTIA
text: For never shall you lie by Portia’s side

speaker: PORTIA
text: With an unquiet soul. You shall have gold

speaker: PORTIA
text: To pay the petty debt twenty times over.

speaker: PORTIA
text: When it is paid, bring your true friend along.

speaker: PORTIA
text: My maid Nerissa and myself meantime

speaker: PORTIA
text: Will live as maids and widows. Come, away,

speaker: PORTIA
text: For you shall hence upon your wedding day.

speaker: PORTIA
text: Bid your friends welcome, show a merry cheer;

speaker: PORTIA
text: Since you are dear bought, I will love you dear.

speaker: PORTIA
text: But let me hear the letter of your friend.

speaker: BASSANIO
text:
reads

speaker: PORTIA
text: O love, dispatch all business and begone!

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Since I have your good leave to go away,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I will make haste. But till I come again,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: No bed shall e’er be guilty of my stay,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Nor rest be interposer ’twixt us twain.
